questions.prompt: Ratio of boys to girls in a class with 8 boys and 12 girls in simplest form.
questions.explanation: Worked example: ratio of 8 boys to 12 girls (6.RP.A.1). Write 8:12. Simplify by dividing both parts by their GCF: GCF(8,12) = 4. So 8 ÷ 4 = 2 and 12 ÷ 4 = 3, giving 2:3. This means '2 boys for every 3 girls.' Common student error: writing 3:2 (reversing the order) or 2/3 without acknowledging it's a ratio. Strategy: keep the order matching the question (boys FIRST, girls SECOND). Simplify by dividing BOTH parts by the GCF. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.1 introduces ratio language and notation explicitly.

questions.prompt: A ratio equivalent to 4:6 with both numbers divided by 2.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 4:6 divided by 2 (6.RP.A.3a). Divide BOTH parts by 2: 4 ÷ 2 = 2 and 6 ÷ 2 = 3, giving 2:3. Equivalent ratios represent the same comparison at a different scale. Common student error: dividing only one side (giving 2:6 or 4:3) or adding instead. Strategy: equivalent ratios are produced by MULTIPLYING or DIVIDING both parts by the same nonzero number. To check equivalence, simplify both ratios to lowest terms and compare. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3a tests ratio tables and equivalence directly.

questions.prompt: Number of cups of flour needed if a recipe uses 2 cups of flour for every 3 cups of sugar and you have 9 cups of sugar.
questions.explanation: Worked example: recipe uses 2 cups flour for every 3 cups sugar; given 9 cups sugar, find flour (6.RP.A.3a). Set up ratio: 2:3 = ?:9. Scale factor from 3 to 9 is x3 (since 3 x 3 = 9). Apply the same factor to flour: 2 x 3 = 6 cups. Common student error: writing 4 (just doubling) or 9 (using wrong ratio direction). Strategy: in a ratio table, find the multiplier that scales the GIVEN side to its new value, then apply the SAME multiplier to the unknown side. Always keep the order: flour stays paired with sugar.

questions.prompt: Whether the ratios 6:9 and 8:12 are equivalent.
questions.explanation: Worked example: are 6:9 and 8:12 equivalent? (6.RP.A.3a). Simplify each. 6:9 ÷ GCF 3 = 2:3. 8:12 ÷ GCF 4 = 2:3. Both simplify to 2:3, so YES, equivalent. Alternative: cross-multiply 6 x 12 = 72 and 9 x 8 = 72 — equal cross products confirm equivalence. Common student error: comparing without simplifying, or comparing only one part. Strategy: simplify each ratio to lowest terms; if they match, they are equivalent. Cross-multiplication is a Grade 6 shortcut: a:b = c:d if and only if a x d = b x c.

questions.prompt: Hours needed to drive 270 miles at 45 mph.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 270 miles at 45 mph — find time (6.RP.A.3b). Time = distance ÷ rate: 270 ÷ 45 = 6 hours. Set up: 45 miles per 1 hour scaled to 270 miles. 270 ÷ 45 = 6, so 6 hours of driving. Common student error: writing 270/45 unsimplified or 6.5 (arithmetic error). Strategy: 'rate' problems use the d = r x t structure; solve for time as t = d ÷ r. Always check units: miles ÷ (miles per hour) = hours ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3b introduces unit-rate reasoning in real-world contexts.

Worked example: 12 apples cost $6 — find unit rate (6.RP.A.2). Unit rate = total ÷ quantity = $6 ÷ 12 = $0.50 per apple. Common student error: writing $2 per apple (dividing wrong way) or $72. Strategy: a unit rate is 'cost per ONE item' or 'distance per ONE hour.' Always divide TOTAL by QUANTITY to find a unit rate. The keyword 'per' signals division. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.2 introduces unit rates and contrasts them with ratios — every unit rate is a ratio whose second term is 1.

questions.prompt: Miles per hour if a car travels 150 miles in 3 hours.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 150 miles in 3 hours — find mph (6.RP.A.2). Speed = distance ÷ time: 150 ÷ 3 = 50 miles per hour. Common student error: multiplying (150 x 3 = 450) or writing 50 hours. Strategy: 'miles per hour' is a unit rate where 'per' signals division. Total distance (150 mi) ÷ total time (3 hr) = 50 mph. Always check units: miles ÷ hours = miles/hour ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.2 uses speed as the most common real-world unit-rate context.

questions.prompt: Point on the graph y = 4x when x = 3.
questions.explanation: Worked example: y = 4x, find point at x = 3 (6.RP.A.3). Substitute x = 3 into y = 4x: y = 4 x 3 = 12. So the point is (3, 12). Common student error: writing (4, 3) (using the coefficient as a coordinate) or (3, 7). Strategy: proportional relationships y = kx pass through the origin (0, 0) and have constant slope k. To find any point, plug an x-value into the equation and compute the corresponding y-value. The coordinate pair is (x, y) — in order.

questions.prompt: Better deal: $4 for 8 ounces or $5 for 12 ounces.
questions.explanation: Worked example: compare $4 for 8 oz vs. $5 for 12 oz (6.RP.A.3b). Compute each unit rate: $4 ÷ 8 oz = $0.50/oz; $5 ÷ 12 oz ≈ $0.417/oz. Smaller cost per ounce is better — so $5 for 12 oz is the better deal. Common student error: comparing total cost ($4 vs $5) and choosing $4, or comparing total ounces. Strategy: 'better deal' problems require unit-rate comparison. Compute cost per single unit (oz, lb, item) for each option; pick the SMALLEST cost per unit. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3b tests this directly.

questions.prompt: Pages per minute if a student reads 60 pages in 24 minutes.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 60 pages in 24 minutes — find pages per minute (6.RP.A.2). Unit rate = 60 ÷ 24 = 2.5 pages per minute. Common student error: dividing the other way (24 ÷ 60 = 0.4, gives minutes per page) or writing 60/24 unsimplified. Strategy: 'pages per minute' means pages ÷ minutes (the unit BEFORE 'per' is on top). Always divide the quantity in the numerator by the quantity in the denominator. Reverse-check: at 2.5 pages/min for 24 min = 60 pages ✓.

questions.prompt: Next pair in the ratio table that starts 2:3, 4:6, 6:9.
questions.explanation: Worked example: ratio table 2:3, 4:6, 6:9 — next pair (6.RP.A.3a). Pattern: each pair is the previous scaled by +2 on the left side (2,4,6,...) and +3 on the right (3,6,9,...). Or each pair is k times (2,3): k=1,2,3,... — so next is 4 x (2,3) = (8,12). Common student error: writing 8:11 (adding 2 to left, 2 to right — wrong scaling) or 10:13. Strategy: in a ratio table, each row scales the original ratio by the same factor. Find the scale factor from one row to the next; apply uniformly.

questions.prompt: Number of red marbles in a jar where the ratio of red to blue is 3:5 and there are 25 blue.
questions.explanation: Worked example: red:blue = 3:5, 25 blue — find red (6.RP.A.3a). Scale factor for blue: 25 ÷ 5 = 5. Apply same factor to red: 3 x 5 = 15 red marbles. Common student error: writing 5 (treating the ratio as 1:5 instead of 3:5) or 12.5 (dividing 25/2). Strategy: in proportion problems, find the multiplier that turns the GIVEN ratio part into its actual value, then apply that multiplier to the unknown ratio part. Always keep parts in the same column (red with red, blue with blue).

questions.prompt: Total students if the ratio of staff to students is 1:18 and there are 4 staff.
questions.explanation: Worked example: staff:students = 1:18, 4 staff — find students (6.RP.A.3a). Scale factor for staff: 4 ÷ 1 = 4. Apply to students: 18 x 4 = 72 students. Common student error: writing 22 (adding 4 + 18) or 18 (forgetting to scale). Strategy: 1:18 means one staff per 18 students. With 4 staff, multiply 18 by 4 to get total students. Always preserve the order in the ratio (staff first, students second) and scale BOTH parts by the same factor.

questions.prompt: Earnings for 8 hours at $12 per hour.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 8 hours at $12 per hour (6.RP.A.3b). Total earnings = rate x time = $12 x 8 = $96. Common student error: writing $20 (adding 8 + 12) or $1.50 (dividing). Strategy: 'per hour' is a unit rate signaling multiplication for total. To find total earnings from a rate, multiply: rate x time = total. Reverse: total ÷ time = rate (unit-rate problem). NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3b tests both directions — given any two of {rate, time, total}, find the third.

Worked example: 5:8 = 15:? (6.RP.A.3a). Scale factor: 15 ÷ 5 = 3. Apply to 8: 8 x 3 = 24. So the unknown is 24, giving 5:8 = 15:24. Common student error: writing 18 (adding 3 to 8 instead of multiplying) or 13 (using a wrong scale factor). Strategy: in proportional problems, find the scale factor by dividing the known part of the second ratio by the corresponding part of the first ratio. Apply the SAME factor to the unknown. Cross-multiplication is an alternative check: 5 x ? = 8 x 15 → ? = 24.

questions.prompt: Each part's value when 24 is split into a ratio of 1:2 (two parts).
questions.explanation: Worked example: split 24 into a 1:2 ratio (6.RP.A.3a). Total parts = 1 + 2 = 3. Each part = 24 ÷ 3 = 8. So the two groups are 1 x 8 = 8 and 2 x 8 = 16. Each part of the tape diagram equals 8. Common student error: writing 12 (treating 1:2 as splitting in half) or 24/2 = 12. Strategy: 'split in a ratio a:b' means total = a + b parts. Find the value of one part by dividing the total by (a+b), then multiply by each ratio value.

questions.prompt: Value of the larger group when 30 is split in a 2:3 ratio.
questions.explanation: Worked example: split 30 in a 2:3 ratio — find the LARGER group (6.RP.A.3a). Total parts = 2 + 3 = 5. Each part = 30 ÷ 5 = 6. Larger group = 3 x 6 = 18. Smaller group = 2 x 6 = 12 (check: 18 + 12 = 30 ✓). Common student error: writing 20 (taking 2/3 of 30 — wrong fraction) or 18 from a 3:2 interpretation. Strategy: total parts (a+b) divides the whole; each ratio part has size (whole ÷ total parts). Multiply by the larger ratio number for the larger group.

questions.prompt: Number of girls if a class has a 3:4 boys-to-girls ratio and 21 boys.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 3:4 boys-to-girls, 21 boys — find girls (6.RP.A.3a). Scale factor for boys: 21 ÷ 3 = 7. Apply to girls: 4 x 7 = 28 girls. Common student error: writing 24 (adding 21 + 3) or 25 (just adding 21 + 4). Strategy: in ratio problems with one known value, find the multiplier from the known side, then apply uniformly. 3:4 means 'for every 3 boys, 4 girls' — with 21 boys (seven groups of 3), there are 7 groups of 4 girls = 28.

questions.prompt: Difference between two parts of a 5:8 ratio if the total is 65.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 5:8 ratio, total = 65 — find the difference between parts (6.RP.A.3a). Total parts = 5 + 8 = 13. Each part = 65 ÷ 13 = 5. Larger group = 8 x 5 = 40; smaller group = 5 x 5 = 25. Difference = 40 − 25 = 15. Common student error: writing 3 (subtracting 8 − 5 = 3 from the ratio itself) or 30. Strategy: convert ratio parts to actual values (multiply by the unit value), then compute the requested quantity (difference, sum, larger, etc.). Always verify totals add to the original whole.

questions.prompt: Smaller share when $84 is split in a 4:3 ratio.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $84 in a 4:3 ratio — find the SMALLER share (6.RP.A.3a). Total parts = 4 + 3 = 7. Each part = $84 ÷ 7 = $12. Smaller share = 3 x $12 = $36. Larger = 4 x $12 = $48 (check: $36 + $48 = $84 ✓). Common student error: writing $48 (the larger share) or $42 ($84 ÷ 2 — ignoring the ratio). Strategy: identify the smaller and larger ratio numbers; the smaller fraction times the total is the smaller share. Find unit-value first by dividing total by sum of ratio parts.

questions.prompt: Coordinate pair from a ratio table with x = 1, y = 5.
questions.explanation: Worked example: from ratio table x=1, y=5, the coordinate pair is (1, 5) (6.RP.A.3a). The first coordinate is x; the second is y. Common student error: writing (5, 1) (swapping). Strategy: ordered pair = (x, y), x before y, alphabetical order. Every row of a ratio table corresponds to a point on the proportional graph. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3a uses coordinate plots of ratio tables as a foundational representation of proportional relationships.

questions.prompt: Origin of every proportional relationship's graph.
questions.explanation: Worked example: every proportional relationship passes through the ORIGIN (0, 0) (6.RP.A.3a). This is because y = kx evaluates to y = k x 0 = 0 when x = 0. Common student error: thinking proportional graphs can start at any point, or confusing with non-proportional linear functions y = mx + b (which only pass through origin when b = 0). Strategy: proportional graphs are STRAIGHT LINES through (0, 0). If a line doesn't pass through the origin, the relationship is NOT proportional. This is a key Grade 6 6.RP.A distinction.

Worked example: (1, 6), (2, 12), (3, 18) — find constant of proportionality (6.RP.A.3a). Compute y/x for each point: 6/1 = 6; 12/2 = 6; 18/3 = 6. Constant of proportionality k = 6. Equation: y = 6x. Common student error: writing 6, 12, 18 (the y-values) or using a single point to draw a conclusion without checking. Strategy: in a proportional relationship, y/x is the same for every point. This constant ratio is called the 'constant of proportionality' (k). Always test multiple points to confirm proportionality.

questions.prompt: Hours needed at $15 per hour to earn $180.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $15 per hour, $180 total — find hours (6.RP.A.3b). Hours = total ÷ rate = $180 ÷ $15 = 12 hours. Common student error: multiplying instead ($180 x $15) or writing $2,700. Strategy: 'per hour' is a unit rate. To find time from total and rate: time = total ÷ rate. Always check units: dollars ÷ (dollars per hour) = hours ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3b expects students to solve rate problems flexibly in either direction.

questions.prompt: Equation form for a proportional relationship with constant k.
questions.explanation: Worked example: equation for proportional relationship with constant k is y = kx (6.RP.A.3a). The constant k is the unit rate or 'constant of proportionality.' Common student error: writing y = k + x (additive instead of multiplicative) or y = k/x (inverse). Strategy: PROPORTIONAL means y is a constant MULTIPLE of x — that's y = kx. The graph is a straight line through the origin with slope k. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3 expects fluent transitions between table, graph, and equation y = kx.

Worked example: 1/2 as a percent (6.RP.A.3c). 'Percent' means 'per hundred,' so we need n/100 = 1/2. Scale 1/2 to denominator 100: 1/2 x 50/50 = 50/100. So 1/2 = 50%. Common student error: writing 1.5% or 0.5% (decimal/percent confusion). Strategy: to convert fraction → percent, scale the fraction to denominator 100. Alternative: divide top by bottom, multiply by 100. 1 ÷ 2 = 0.5; 0.5 x 100 = 50%. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c expects fluent fraction↔decimal↔percent triangle.

Worked example: 0.75 as a percent (6.RP.A.3c). Multiply by 100: 0.75 x 100 = 75%. Common student error: writing 0.75% or 7.5%. Strategy: decimal → percent means multiply by 100 (or shift decimal point TWO places to the right). 0.75 → 75. (decimal moves to make 75.0%). Reverse: percent → decimal means divide by 100 (shift decimal point two places LEFT). Both directions are core Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c skills.

Worked example: 25% as a decimal (6.RP.A.3c). Divide by 100: 25 ÷ 100 = 0.25. Or shift decimal point two places LEFT: 25.0 → 0.25. Common student error: writing 0.025 (one shift too many) or 2.5. Strategy: percent → decimal = divide by 100 (shift two places left). Percent → fraction: write as n/100, then simplify. 25% = 25/100 = 1/4. Always double-check by reversing: 0.25 x 100 = 25% ✓. Verify by reversing: 0.25 x 100 = 25%. The decimal point 'jumps' two places between percent and decimal forms because percent literally means 'out of 100.'

questions.prompt: Fraction equivalent of 40% in simplest form.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 40% as a fraction in simplest form (6.RP.A.3c). Write as 40/100; simplify by dividing both parts by GCF 20: 40 ÷ 20 = 2; 100 ÷ 20 = 5. So 40% = 2/5. Common student error: writing 40/100 unsimplified, or 4/10 (didn't fully simplify). Strategy: percent → fraction means write as n/100 and reduce to lowest terms. Find GCF and divide both top and bottom. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c expects simplest-form answers in percent-to-fraction conversions.

Worked example: 3/8 as a percent (6.RP.A.3c). Divide: 3 ÷ 8 = 0.375. Multiply by 100: 0.375 x 100 = 37.5%. Common student error: writing 3.8% or 37% (rounding too early). Strategy: when the denominator doesn't divide cleanly into 100, convert by dividing (decimal), then multiplying by 100 (percent). Some fractions produce decimal percents (37.5%) — that's fine. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c includes fractions like 1/8, 3/8, 5/8, 7/8 which yield decimal percents.

Worked example: 10% of 80 (6.RP.A.3c). Convert 10% to 0.1, then multiply: 0.1 x 80 = 8. Quick mental method: 10% means 'move decimal point one place left,' so 80 → 8.0 = 8. Common student error: writing 0.8 (one too many shifts) or 10. Strategy: 'percent of a number' means PERCENT x NUMBER. Use decimals or fractions. 10% is especially easy — just divide by 10 (shift decimal). For 5% take half of 10%; for 20% double 10%.

Worked example: 20% of 50 (6.RP.A.3c). Convert 20% to 0.2, multiply: 0.2 x 50 = 10. Or: 20% = 1/5, so 50 ÷ 5 = 10. Common student error: writing 1 (computing 10% only) or 30. Strategy: 'X% of Y' = X/100 x Y. Quick conversions: 10% = ÷10, 20% = ÷5, 25% = ÷4, 50% = ÷2, 75% = x3/4, 100% = the whole. Memorizing these simple percent fractions speeds up mental math. To verify, count back: 20% of 50 means '20 out of every 100 of 50,' which is 1/5 of 50 = 10. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c uses these mental-math fraction-percent equivalents heavily.

questions.prompt: Total cost with 8% tax on a $25 item.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $25 item with 8% tax — total cost (6.RP.A.3c). Tax: 8% of $25 = 0.08 x 25 = $2. Total: $25 + $2 = $27. Common student error: writing $33 (using 8 instead of 8% = 0.08) or $25.08. Strategy: tax problems require two steps — compute the tax amount, then ADD to the base price. Some problems ask for tax alone; others ask for total. Read carefully. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c tests tax, tip, and discount problems as the standard real-world application of percents.

Worked example: 25% of $60 (6.RP.A.3c). 25% = 1/4, so $60 ÷ 4 = $15. Alternative: 0.25 x 60 = 15. Common student error: writing $15 without dollar sign or $24 (wrong fraction). Strategy: memorize key percent shortcuts — 25% = ÷4, 50% = ÷2, 10% = ÷10. For 25% of any amount, divide by 4. Quick check: 4 x $15 = $60 ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c expects fluency with these mental-math conversions. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c expects fluency with these mental-math conversions. Quick check: 4 quarters of $60 = $60, so 1 quarter = $15.

Worked example: 15% tip on $40 (6.RP.A.3c). Method 1: 0.15 x 40 = $6. Method 2: 10% of $40 = $4; 5% of $40 = $2; sum = $6. Common student error: writing $40 + $15 = $55 (using 15 instead of 15%) or $0.60. Strategy: tip-and-tax problems use 'percent of a number.' Common mental method: compute 10% (easy — shift decimal), then halve to get 5%, then add together for 15%. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c tests these everyday percent applications.

questions.prompt: Discounted price of a $50 item that is 10% off.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $50 item, 10% off (6.RP.A.3c). Discount: 10% of $50 = $5. Sale price: $50 − $5 = $45. Alternative: pay 90% of $50 = 0.9 x 50 = $45. Common student error: writing $40 (applying 10% twice or wrong arithmetic) or $5 (the discount, not the final price). Strategy: discount problems have two valid setups — (1) compute discount, then SUBTRACT from original; (2) compute (100% − discount%) of original directly. Both methods give the same final price.

questions.prompt: Original price if 20% off equals $16 saved.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 20% off saves $16 — find original price (6.RP.A.3c). 20% of original = $16. So original = $16 ÷ 0.20 = $80. Check: 20% of $80 = $16 ✓. Common student error: writing $16/20 = $0.80 (shifted decimal wrong) or $32. Strategy: 'percent of unknown' problems: if X% of N = K, then N = K ÷ (X/100). Set up an equation, then divide to solve. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c tests reverse-percent problems frequently.

questions.prompt: Final cost if a $40 item is 25% off plus 10% tax on the discounted price.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $40 item, 25% off + 10% tax on discounted price (6.RP.A.3c). Discount: 25% of $40 = $10. Sale price: $40 − $10 = $30. Tax: 10% of $30 = $3. Final: $30 + $3 = $33. Common student error: applying tax to original $40 ($30 + $4 = $34) or combining 25% − 10% = 15% off (incorrect — different bases). Strategy: multi-step percent problems must use the CORRECT base for each step. Discount uses original; tax usually uses discounted price (unless stated otherwise).

questions.prompt: Final amount after a $200 investment grows 15%.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $200 investment grows 15% — find new value (6.RP.A.3c). Growth: 15% of $200 = $30. New value: $200 + $30 = $230. Or directly: 115% of $200 = 1.15 x 200 = $230. Common student error: writing $230 with wrong arithmetic, or $30 (just the growth). Strategy: percent INCREASE problems: new = original + (% x original) OR new = (100% + %) x original. Both forms work. Always verify direction (increase = up, decrease = down).

Worked example: $250 purchase, 7% tax (6.RP.A.3c). Tax: 7% of $250 = 0.07 x 250 = $17.50. Common student error: writing $17 (truncated incorrectly) or $250.07 (added decimal wrong). Strategy: tax is computed as PERCENT x BASE. Convert percent to decimal (7% = 0.07), multiply by the base ($250). Always keep two decimal places for money. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c expects fluent computation of sales tax in real-world contexts.

questions.prompt: Fraction shown in a 10x10 grid with 30 squares shaded.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 10x10 grid with 30 squares shaded (6.RP.A.3c). Total squares = 100; shaded = 30. Fraction shaded = 30/100 = 30%. Common student error: writing 30 (without /100) or 3/10 unsimplified-as-a-percent. Strategy: a 10x10 grid has 100 cells, so shaded cells DIRECTLY represent the percent. 30 of 100 = 30%. This is the conceptual base for percent — 'per hundred.' NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c uses 10x10 grids as the standard percent model.

questions.prompt: Percent represented by 7 of 10 equal parts shaded.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 7 of 10 parts shaded — find percent (6.RP.A.3c). Fraction = 7/10. Scale to denominator 100: 7/10 x 10/10 = 70/100 = 70%. Common student error: writing 7% (treating 7/10 as 7/100) or 0.7% (decimal confusion). Strategy: 'X out of 10' converts to percent by multiplying X by 10 (since 10 → 100 is x10). 7 of 10 = 70%; 9 of 10 = 90%; 3 of 10 = 30%. Always scale fractions to a denominator of 100 to convert to percent.

questions.prompt: Number of cells to shade on a 10x10 grid to show 45%.
questions.explanation: Worked example: shade 45% of a 10x10 grid (6.RP.A.3c). 45% means 45 out of 100, so shade 45 cells. Common student error: writing 4.5 cells (impossible) or 4 cells (forgetting the percent is 'out of 100'). Strategy: a 10x10 grid has exactly 100 cells, so percent → cells is direct: 45% = 45 cells. This visualization makes 'percent = per hundred' concrete. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c uses these grid models as introductions to percent reasoning.

questions.prompt: Percent of a double number line if one mark is at 40 and ends at 80.
questions.explanation: Worked example: double number line, one mark at 40, end at 80 — find percent (6.RP.A.3c). 40 is HALF of 80, so 40 represents 50%. Common student error: writing 40% (using the value as the percent) or 2/3. Strategy: in a double number line, the bottom line shows percent (0% to 100%) and the top shows values (0 to whole). Find the ratio of the marked value to the end value: 40/80 = 1/2 = 50%. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c uses double number lines for proportional percent reasoning.

questions.prompt: Total value if 30% on a bar diagram represents 21.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 30% of unknown total = 21 — find total (6.RP.A.3c). 30% x total = 21. Solve: total = 21 ÷ 0.30 = 70. Check: 30% of 70 = 21 ✓. Common student error: writing 21 + 30 = 51 or 21 x 30/100 = 6.3. Strategy: when a percent x unknown = known value, divide the known value by the decimal form of the percent to find the unknown. Or use proportion: 30/100 = 21/x → 30x = 2100 → x = 70. Alternative: use a percent bar — 30% labeled 21, 100% labeled ?. Scale up: 100/30 = 10/3, so 21 x 10/3 = 70. Both methods give the same answer.

questions.prompt: Price after a $10 item increases by 20%.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $10 item up 20% (6.RP.A.3c). Increase: 20% of $10 = $2. New price: $10 + $2 = $12. Or: 120% of $10 = $12. Common student error: writing $30 (treating 20% as 20) or $8 (subtracting). Strategy: percent INCREASE = original + (% x original) = (100% + %) x original. Verify direction — 'increase' or 'markup' = up, while 'decrease' or 'discount' = down. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c uses markup as a key real-world percent application.

questions.prompt: Price after a $50 item is marked up 50%.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $50 marked up 50% (6.RP.A.3c). Markup: 50% of $50 = $25. New price: $50 + $25 = $75. Or: 150% of $50 = 1.5 x $50 = $75. Common student error: writing $100 (doubling) or $25 (just the markup). Strategy: a 50% markup ADDS 50% to the original (NOT doubles it). The new price is 1.5 times the original. Always check: 50% markup on any amount = original x 1.5. Reverse: $75 ÷ 1.5 = $50 ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c uses markup/markdown as a foundational percent application. Common business contexts include retail pricing, tips, and sales tax.

questions.prompt: Salary after a $40,000 worker gets a 10% raise.
questions.explanation: Worked example: $40,000 salary, 10% raise (6.RP.A.3c). Raise: 10% of $40,000 = $4,000. New salary: $40,000 + $4,000 = $44,000. Common student error: writing $50,000 (adding 10,000) or $36,000 (subtracting). Strategy: a 10% raise INCREASES salary by 10% of the CURRENT salary. Compute the raise as 10% x salary, then add. Quick method: 10% of $40,000 = $4,000 (shift decimal one place left). NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c includes raises and pay scales as percent applications.

questions.prompt: Percent of students absent if 18 of 200 students are absent.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 18 absent of 200 — find percent (6.RP.A.3c). 18 ÷ 200 = 0.09 = 9%. Or scale to 100: 18/200 = 9/100 = 9%. Common student error: writing 18% (using the count as percent) or 0.09% (decimal confusion). Strategy: 'what percent' problems use PART ÷ WHOLE x 100. Here 18 ÷ 200 = 0.09 → multiply by 100 → 9%. Always reduce the fraction first (18/200 = 9/100) for cleaner mental math. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c tests these 'what percent' problems frequently.

Worked example: $80 to $100 — percent change (6.RP.A.3c). Change = $100 − $80 = $20. Percent change = change ÷ ORIGINAL = $20 ÷ $80 = 0.25 = 25%. So price increased 25%. Common student error: dividing by the NEW value ($100) to get 20%, or by the difference ($20). Strategy: percent change = (new − old) ÷ OLD x 100. The DENOMINATOR is always the ORIGINAL/STARTING value, not the new one. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c is precise about this — always use the original as the base.

Worked example: 2.4 + 3.6 (6.NS.B.3). Line up decimal points and add column by column. Tenths: 4 + 6 = 10 (write 0, carry 1). Ones: 2 + 3 + 1 = 6. Decimal point drops down. Answer: 6.0 = 6. Common student error: writing 5.10 (forgetting to carry) or 6.10. Strategy: stack decimals vertically with decimal points aligned. Regroup like whole-number addition. Drop trailing zeros after the decimal for simplest form. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.3 expects fluent multi-digit decimal arithmetic.

Worked example: 8.5 − 2.78 (6.NS.B.3). Pad with a zero so 8.5 becomes 8.50. Subtract: hundredths 0−8 borrow → 10−8=2; tenths now 4; 4−7 borrow → 14−7=7; tenths now 7; ones 7−2=5. Wait, careful: 8.50 − 2.78: borrow tenths to hundredths to get 14 tens; subtract 8 from 14 = 6... Let me redo. 8.50 − 2.78: hundredths 0−8 → borrow, 10−8=2; tenths becomes 4; 4−7 → borrow, 14−7=7; ones 7−2=5. Result: 5.72. Common student error: 5.78 (not borrowing) or 6.78. Strategy: pad with zeros so decimals match; borrow column-by-column.

Worked example: 0.4 x 0.3 (6.NS.B.3). Multiply digits: 4 x 3 = 12. Count total decimal places in factors: 0.4 has 1; 0.3 has 1; total 2 places. Place decimal in product: 0.12. Common student error: writing 1.2 (one place) or 12 (no places). Strategy: ignore decimals while multiplying, then count total decimal digits in BOTH factors and place that many decimal places in the product. Pad with leading zeros if needed. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.3 tests this directly.

Worked example: 4.5 ÷ 0.5 (6.NS.B.3). Multiply both numbers by 10 to clear the divisor's decimal: 4.5 ÷ 0.5 = 45 ÷ 5 = 9. Common student error: writing 0.9 (forgetting to shift dividend) or 4.5/0.5 = 4 (mishandling decimals). Strategy: shift the decimal in the DIVISOR until it disappears; shift the DIVIDEND by the same number of places; then divide as whole numbers. Place the decimal point straight up in the quotient. Check: 0.5 x 9 = 4.5 ✓.

Worked example: 7/8 as a decimal (6.NS.B.3). Divide 7 ÷ 8: 7.000 ÷ 8 = 0.875 (since 8 x 0.875 = 7). Common student error: writing 0.78 (terminating early) or 7.8 (treating as a mixed number). Strategy: fraction → decimal means top ÷ bottom. Use long division, adding zeros after the decimal point as needed. Some fractions terminate (7/8 = 0.875); others repeat (1/3 = 0.333...). NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.3 expects fluent fraction-to-decimal conversion.

Worked example: 1/4 + 1/4 (6.NS.A.1 / 5.NF.A.1). Same denominator — add numerators: 1 + 1 = 2; keep denominator 4. Sum = 2/4. Simplify: divide top and bottom by GCF 2: 1/2. Common student error: writing 2/8 (adding bottoms too) or leaving 2/4 unsimplified. Strategy: with same denominators, just add numerators. Always simplify the final answer by dividing top and bottom by their GCF. NYS Grade 6 expects fraction operations from Grade 5 to be automatic.

Worked example: 5/6 − 1/3 (6.NS.A.1). LCD of 6 and 3 is 6. Convert 1/3 = 2/6. Subtract: 5/6 − 2/6 = 3/6. Simplify: 3/6 = 1/2 (divide by GCF 3). Common student error: writing 4/3 (subtracting bottoms) or 3/6 unsimplified. Strategy: find LCD, convert each fraction, subtract numerators, ALWAYS simplify. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 builds on Grade 5 fraction fluency. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 expects fluent fraction subtraction with unlike denominators. Always check that the final answer is in simplest form by dividing top and bottom by their GCF.

Worked example: 4 ÷ 1/3 (6.NS.A.1). Use KCF: 4 ÷ 1/3 = 4 x 3/1 = 12. Visual: 'how many 1/3s fit in 4?' Each whole has 3 thirds, so 4 wholes = 12 thirds. Common student error: writing 4/3 (multiplying instead) or 1.33. Strategy: dividing by a unit fraction 1/n gives the number times n. Use 'keep, change, flip' (KCF): keep the whole, change ÷ to x, flip 1/3 to 3. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 fully introduces fraction-by-fraction division.

Worked example: 5 1/3 − 2 2/3 (6.NS.A.1). LCD = 3. 5 1/3 has fractional part 1/3, which is SMALLER than 2/3, so borrow: 5 1/3 = 4 + 1 + 1/3 = 4 + 3/3 + 1/3 = 4 4/3. Now subtract: 4 4/3 − 2 2/3 = 2 2/3. Common student error: writing 3 −1/3 (negative fraction) or 2 1/3 (subtracting fractions wrong). Strategy: when the second fraction is bigger than the first, BORROW 1 from the whole and convert to the denominator (1 = 3/3).

questions.prompt: Servings if a 7 1/2 cup jar has 3/4 cup per serving.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 7 1/2 cups ÷ 3/4 cup per serving (6.NS.A.1). Convert mixed: 7 1/2 = 15/2. Use KCF: 15/2 ÷ 3/4 = 15/2 x 4/3 = 60/6 = 10. So 10 servings. Common student error: writing 5 (only using 7 ÷ 3/4 or other partial calculation). Strategy: convert mixed numbers to improper fractions first. Use KCF for division. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 introduces fraction-by-fraction division in real-world contexts.

Worked example: 3 1/2 as improper fraction (6.NS.A.1 / 5.NF.B.3). Multiply whole x denominator: 3 x 2 = 6. Add numerator: 6 + 1 = 7. Keep denominator: 7/2. Check: 7 ÷ 2 = 3 r 1 = 3 1/2 ✓. Common student error: writing 31/2 (concatenating) or 4/2 = 2. Strategy: improper = (whole x denom + numer) / denom. Memorize the formula. Always verify by dividing back to a mixed number. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 expects fluent mixed↔improper conversion. Quick visual: 3 1/2 = 3 wholes + 1 half = 6 halves + 1 half = 7 halves = 7/2.

Worked example: 9/4 as mixed number (6.NS.A.1). Divide: 9 ÷ 4 = 2 remainder 1. Whole part = 2; fractional part = 1/4. So 9/4 = 2 1/4. Common student error: writing 9 1/4 (using numerator as whole) or 2 4/1. Strategy: divide numerator by denominator. Quotient = whole part; remainder = new numerator; original denominator stays. Verify: 2 1/4 = 2 + 1/4 = 8/4 + 1/4 = 9/4 ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 builds on this skill for fraction subtraction with regrouping. Always verify by converting back: 2 1/4 = 2 x 4 + 1 = 9, so 9/4 ✓.

Worked example: 1 1/2 + 2 1/4 (6.NS.A.1). LCD = 4. Convert: 1 1/2 = 1 2/4. Add wholes: 1 + 2 = 3. Add fractions: 2/4 + 1/4 = 3/4. Total: 3 3/4. Common student error: writing 3 2/6 (adding wrong) or 3 3/8. Strategy: add whole parts and fraction parts separately. Use a common denominator for fractions. If fractions sum ≥ 1, carry into the whole part. Alternative method: convert both to improper fractions (1 1/2 = 3/2; 2 1/4 = 9/4), find LCD (4), convert (3/2 = 6/4), add (6/4 + 9/4 = 15/4 = 3 3/4). Both methods give 3 3/4.

questions.prompt: Cups of flour in 1/2 of a recipe needing 3 1/2 cups.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1/2 of a recipe needing 3 1/2 cups flour (6.NS.A.1). Compute 1/2 x 3 1/2. Convert mixed: 3 1/2 = 7/2. Multiply: 1/2 x 7/2 = 7/4 = 1 3/4 cups. Common student error: writing 1 3/4 by accident or 7/4 unsimplified-as-improper. Strategy: 'half of a recipe' = recipe x 1/2. Convert mixed numbers to improper fractions, multiply, then convert back. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 uses recipe-scaling as a standard fraction-multiplication context.

Worked example: 2 1/3 x 3 (6.NS.A.1). Convert mixed: 2 1/3 = 7/3. Multiply: 7/3 x 3 = 21/3 = 7. Common student error: writing 6 1/3 (multiplying whole part only) or 7 1/3. Strategy: convert mixed numbers to improper fractions FIRST, then multiply. Don't multiply whole parts and fractional parts separately — you'll miss cross-terms. Verify: 2 1/3 + 2 1/3 + 2 1/3 = 6 + 3/3 = 6 + 1 = 7 ✓. The multiplication 2 1/3 x 3 means 'three copies of 2 1/3,' which equals 7. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 includes mixed-number multiplication as a key fluency.

Worked example: GCF of 12 and 18 (6.NS.B.4). Factors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Factors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Common factors: 1,2,3,6. Greatest = 6. So GCF(12,18) = 6. Common student error: writing 36 (LCM instead of GCF) or 2 (a common but not the greatest). Strategy: list factors of each number, find shared ones, pick the LARGEST. Alternative: prime factorization — 12 = 2²·3; 18 = 2·3². GCF = product of common primes at lowest power = 2·3 = 6.

Worked example: LCM of 4 and 6 (6.NS.B.4). Multiples of 4: 4,8,12,16,20,24. Multiples of 6: 6,12,18,24. Common multiples: 12,24,... Smallest = 12. So LCM(4,6) = 12. Common student error: writing 24 (a common multiple, not the LEAST) or 10 (just adding 4+6). Strategy: list multiples of each, find shared ones, pick the SMALLEST. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.4 expects fluent LCM for use in fraction addition. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.4 expects fluent LCM for use in fraction addition. Verify: 12 is divisible by both 4 and 6 (12/4 = 3, 12/6 = 2). No smaller positive number works.

Worked example: prime factorization of 24 (6.NS.B.4). Divide by primes: 24 ÷ 2 = 12; 12 ÷ 2 = 6; 6 ÷ 2 = 3; 3 ÷ 3 = 1. So 24 = 2 x 2 x 2 x 3 = 2³ x 3. Common student error: writing 4 x 6 (not prime) or 2 x 12 (not all primes). Strategy: keep dividing by primes (2, 3, 5, 7, ...) until you reach 1. The list of primes used (with repeats) is the prime factorization. Use a factor tree for visual clarity.

Worked example: GCF of 36 and 60 (6.NS.B.4). Prime factorization: 36 = 2²·3²; 60 = 2²·3·5. Common primes at lowest power: 2² · 3 = 4 x 3 = 12. So GCF(36,60) = 12. Common student error: writing 6 (incomplete) or 60 (wrong direction). Strategy: prime factorization is reliable for large numbers. Identify primes appearing in BOTH numbers, take the LOWER power for each, multiply. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.4 expects students to handle two-digit GCF problems efficiently.

Worked example: LCM of 8 and 12 (6.NS.B.4). Prime factorization: 8 = 2³; 12 = 2²·3. LCM = take HIGHEST power of each prime: 2³ · 3 = 8 x 3 = 24. So LCM(8,12) = 24. Common student error: writing 96 (multiplying numbers) or 4 (GCF instead). Strategy: for LCM, take each prime's HIGHEST power across all numbers and multiply. For GCF, take each prime's LOWEST power. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.4 expects GCF and LCM up to two-digit numbers.

questions.prompt: Total length of 5 boards each 2.4 m long.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 5 boards x 2.4 m each (6.NS.B.3). 5 x 2.4 = 12 meters. Common student error: writing 12.0 m and dropping the unit, or 5.24. Strategy: 'each' is a multiplication keyword. Multiply number of items x size per item = total. Check units: items x meters/item = meters ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.3 expects fluent decimal multiplication in real-world contexts like length, weight, and money. The 'each' keyword signals multiplication. Quick check: 5 boards of 2.4 m each is the same as adding 2.4 + 2.4 + 2.4 + 2.4 + 2.4 = 12 m.

questions.prompt: Pieces of pizza per person if 3 pizzas with 8 slices each are shared among 6.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 3 pizzas x 8 slices ÷ 6 people (6.NS.B.3). Total slices: 3 x 8 = 24. Per person: 24 ÷ 6 = 4 slices. Common student error: dividing pizzas by people (3 ÷ 6 = 0.5) or skipping the multiplication step. Strategy: two-step problems — find total first (multiplication), then divide. Always identify total quantity and how it's shared. 'Per person' is the unit-rate keyword for division. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.3 uses pizza/sharing problems for both multiplication and division contexts.

Worked example: 2/3 x 3/4 (6.NS.A.1). Multiply tops, multiply bottoms: (2x3)/(3x4) = 6/12. Simplify by dividing both by GCF 6: 1/2. Or cancel BEFORE multiplying: 2/3 x 3/4 = 2/4 (3s cancel) = 1/2. Common student error: adding (5/7) or only simplifying numerator. Strategy: cross-cancel before multiplying when numerator of one matches denominator of the other. Multiply across, then simplify. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 expects fluent fraction multiplication. Always check whether the result simplifies — here 6/12 has GCF 6, giving 1/2 in simplest form.

Worked example: 3/4 ÷ 2/5 (6.NS.A.1). Use KCF: 3/4 ÷ 2/5 = 3/4 x 5/2 = 15/8. As mixed: 15/8 = 1 7/8. Common student error: writing 6/20 (multiplying without flipping) or 5/6. Strategy: KCF (keep, change, flip) for fraction division. Keep 3/4, change ÷ to x, flip 2/5 to 5/2. Multiply, simplify, optionally convert to mixed. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 introduces fraction-by-fraction division as a key new skill.

questions.prompt: Total cost of 1.5 lb of apples at $2.40/lb.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1.5 lb apples at $2.40/lb (6.NS.B.3). Cost = pounds x price/lb = 1.5 x 2.40 = $3.60. Common student error: writing $24 (forgetting decimal placement) or $3.6 (one decimal place for money). Strategy: 'price per pound' x 'pounds' = total cost. Multiply decimals ignoring decimal points (15 x 24 = 360), then place decimal: 1.5 has 1 place + 2.40 has 2 places = 3 total decimal places: 0.360 → $3.60 (money always two decimals).

Worked example: opposite of −7 (6.NS.C.6). The OPPOSITE flips the sign: −7 → +7. Common student error: writing −7 again or 0. Strategy: 'opposite' means same distance from 0 but opposite direction. Opposite of any negative is positive; opposite of any positive is negative. Opposite of 0 is 0. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.6 introduces integers with absolute value and opposites as foundational concepts. Visual: on a number line, −7 is 7 units LEFT of 0; its opposite +7 is 7 units RIGHT of 0. Opposites are mirror images across zero.

Worked example: −8 + (−6) (6.NS.C.5/6.EE.A.3). Two negatives ADD their absolute values and keep the negative sign: |−8| + |−6| = 8 + 6 = 14; sign stays negative → −14. Common student error: writing −2 (subtracting) or +14 (sign confusion). Strategy: 'same sign' add absolute values, keep sign. 'Different signs' subtract absolute values, take sign of the LARGER absolute value. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C uses these rules for all integer addition.

Worked example: 4 − (−3) (6.NS.C.5). Subtracting a negative is the same as adding the positive: 4 − (−3) = 4 + 3 = 7. Common student error: writing 1 (treating subtraction as subtraction) or −7. Strategy: 'subtracting a negative' = adding a positive. Two negative signs make a positive: − − = +. So a − (−b) becomes a + b. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.5 introduces this 'subtract a negative' rule. Visual: 4 − (−3) on a number line means 'start at 4, walk away from −3' (i.e., right), so move right by 3 units, ending at 7. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.5 expects fluency with this rule.

Worked example: (−4) x (−3) (6.NS.C). Multiplying two negatives gives a POSITIVE: (−4)(−3) = +12. Common student error: writing −12 (forgetting sign rule) or 7 (adding). Strategy: same signs multiply to POSITIVE; different signs multiply to NEGATIVE. Memorize: (−)(−)=+, (+)(+)=+, (−)(+)=−, (+)(−)=−. The same rules apply to division. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C introduces integer multiplication/division. Visual: think of (−)(−) as 'reversing a reverse,' which yields forward (positive). NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C builds these sign rules for all four operations.

questions.prompt: Total change after 4 days of stock-price changes: −3, +5, −2, +6.
questions.explanation: Worked example: stock changes −3, +5, −2, +6 (6.NS.C.5). Sum: −3 + 5 + (−2) + 6. Group: (−3 + 5) + (−2 + 6) = 2 + 4 = 6. Or: negative total = −3 + (−2) = −5; positive total = 5 + 6 = 11; net = 11 − 5 = +6. Common student error: ignoring signs (3+5+2+6=16) or losing track. Strategy: separate positives and negatives, sum each, then combine using sign rules. Always track signs carefully — a single missed negative ruins the answer.

Worked example: −5 is 5 units LEFT of 0 (6.NS.C.6). On a number line, negatives are to the left of 0, positives to the right. The number's MAGNITUDE (5) gives the distance from 0; the sign (negative) gives the direction. Common student error: writing 'right' or 'on 0.' Strategy: number lines are oriented with 0 in the middle (or left), negatives extending left, positives extending right. Absolute value |−5| = 5 = distance from 0. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.6 expects fluent number-line work with integers.

Worked example: −3 + 5 (6.NS.C.5). Start at −3 on the number line; move RIGHT 5 units: −3 → −2 → −1 → 0 → 1 → 2. End at 2. Common student error: writing −8 (treating as addition of magnitudes) or 8 (ignoring negative). Strategy: 'add a positive' = move RIGHT; 'add a negative' = move LEFT. Different signs SUBTRACT absolute values: |5| − |−3| = 5 − 3 = 2; take sign of larger absolute value (positive 5) → +2. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.5 uses number-line models for integer addition.

questions.prompt: Number between −2 and −5 on the number line.
questions.explanation: Worked example: number between −2 and −5 (6.NS.C.7). On the number line, −5 is FURTHER LEFT than −2. Numbers between them include −3 and −4. (Order from left to right: −5, −4, −3, −2.) Common student error: writing 3 (positive) or −1 (outside the interval). Strategy: on a number line, 'between' means strictly inside the interval. For negative intervals, the value closer to 0 is GREATER. So −2 > −3 > −4 > −5. Pick any integer strictly between −2 and −5.

Worked example: smallest of {−2, 0, −5} (6.NS.C.7). On a number line, the SMALLEST is the LEFTMOST. Compare: −5 is left of −2 (since 5 > 2 in absolute value but signs are negative — bigger absolute value means more negative). −5 < −2 < 0. So smallest = −5. Common student error: writing −2 (smaller absolute value but actually GREATER on number line) or 0. Strategy: for negatives, larger absolute value = SMALLER number. Reverse the magnitude order when comparing negatives.

Worked example: −4 vs. −7 (6.NS.C.7). On a number line, −7 is FURTHER LEFT than −4. Numbers to the right are GREATER. So −4 > −7. Common student error: writing −7 (using absolute-value comparison) or 'equal.' Strategy: when comparing two negatives, the one with SMALLER absolute value is GREATER. |−4| = 4 < 7 = |−7|, so −4 > −7. Always check on a number line if unsure. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.7 tests negative comparisons directly.

Worked example: greatest of {0.5, 0.05, 5} (6.NS.C.7). All positive, so larger absolute value = larger number. 5 > 0.5 > 0.05. Greatest = 5. Common student error: writing 0.5 (the one with the most digits looks 'biggest') or 0.05. Strategy: align decimal places when comparing: 5.00 vs 0.50 vs 0.05. The whole-number part decides first; if equal, compare tenths, then hundredths. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.7 expects fluent decimal comparison.

questions.prompt: Temperature after starting at 10°F and dropping 15°F.
questions.explanation: Worked example: start at 10°F, drop 15°F (6.NS.C). New temp = 10 + (−15) = −5°F. Common student error: writing 5°F (ignoring the negative result) or −15°F. Strategy: 'drop' means subtract, OR add a negative. 10 + (−15) yields a negative because |−15| > |10|. Use number line: start at 10, move LEFT 15 units → −5. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C uses temperature change as a standard negative-integer application.

questions.prompt: Symbol that goes between 3/4 and 0.7 to make a true statement.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 3/4 __ 0.7 (6.NS.C.7). Convert 3/4 to decimal: 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75. Compare: 0.75 > 0.70. So 3/4 > 0.7. Use the symbol '>'. Common student error: writing '<' or '=' (treating 0.75 and 0.7 as equal because '7 > 5'). Strategy: convert mixed forms (fraction, decimal, percent) to a single form for comparison. Decimals are easiest — convert all to decimals and compare. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.7 tests cross-form comparisons.

Worked example: order 0.3, 1/2, −0.2 from least to greatest (6.NS.C.7). Convert 1/2 = 0.5. Now compare: −0.2, 0.3, 0.5. Order: −0.2, 0.3, 1/2 (least to greatest). Negatives come first (smallest). Common student error: writing 1/2, 0.3, −0.2 (greatest first) or ignoring the negative. Strategy: convert to a common form (decimal), then order on a number line. Negatives are always smallest; among positives, smaller decimal value comes first.

questions.prompt: Two integers that are 6 units from zero.
questions.explanation: Worked example: two integers 6 units from zero (6.NS.C.6/7). 'Distance from zero' is absolute value. |6| = 6 and |−6| = 6. So both 6 and −6 are 6 units from zero. Common student error: writing only 6 (missing the negative) or 12. Strategy: distance is always positive, but there are usually TWO numbers at any given distance from zero — one positive, one negative. The two integers at distance d are +d and −d.

Worked example: (3, −2) — find quadrant (6.NS.C.6b). Positive x (3), negative y (−2) → Quadrant IV (bottom-right). Common student error: writing Quadrant II or III (sign confusion). Strategy: memorize sign patterns — I (++) top-right, II (−+) top-left, III (−−) bottom-left, IV (+−) bottom-right. The quadrants count counterclockwise from top-right. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.6b extends Grade 5's first-quadrant work to all four.

questions.prompt: Coordinates of the reflection of (4, 5) across the x-axis.
questions.explanation: Worked example: reflect (4, 5) across x-axis (6.NS.C.6b). Reflecting across the x-axis FLIPS the y-coordinate's sign: (4, 5) → (4, −5). The x-coordinate is unchanged. Common student error: writing (−4, 5) (reflected wrong axis) or (−4, −5). Strategy: reflection across x-axis changes only y's sign; reflection across y-axis changes only x's sign. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.6b introduces reflections in the coordinate plane.

questions.prompt: Coordinates of the reflection of (2, 7) across the y-axis.
questions.explanation: Worked example: reflect (2, 7) across y-axis (6.NS.C.6b). Reflecting across the y-axis FLIPS the x-coordinate's sign: (2, 7) → (−2, 7). The y-coordinate is unchanged. Common student error: writing (2, −7) (wrong axis) or (−2, −7). Strategy: reflection across y-axis changes only x's sign. Mnemonic: 'reflecting across an axis flips the OTHER coordinate.' x-axis reflection → y flips; y-axis reflection → x flips. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.6b expects fluent reflection in all four quadrants.

Worked example: distance from (−3, 4) to (5, 4) (6.NS.C.8). Same y-coordinate (4), so horizontal distance. |5 − (−3)| = |5 + 3| = 8 units. Common student error: writing 2 (subtracting wrong) or −8 (forgetting absolute value). Strategy: for axis-aligned distances, subtract the differing coordinates and take absolute value. Same x → |y₂ − y₁|. Same y → |x₂ − x₁|. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.8 uses absolute value for coordinate-plane distances.

Worked example: (−3, 4) — find quadrant (6.NS.C.6b). Negative x, positive y → Quadrant II (top-left). Common student error: writing Quadrant III (bottom-left, all negative) or I. Strategy: memorize quadrant sign patterns: I (++), II (−+), III (−−), IV (+−). Counterclockwise from top-right. For any point, check signs of both coordinates and match to quadrant. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.6b tests this four-quadrant fluency.

questions.prompt: Bank balance after depositing $50 in an account with −$30 balance.
questions.explanation: Worked example: deposit $50 into account with balance −$30 (6.NS.C.5). New balance: −30 + 50 = 20. So balance = $20. Common student error: writing −$80 (treating signs both as negatives) or $80 (ignoring the negative). Strategy: deposits ADD (positive change); withdrawals SUBTRACT (or add negative). Starting with negative balance + positive deposit: result depends on which has bigger absolute value. Here |50| > |−30|, so result is positive. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.5 uses banking contexts for integer arithmetic.

questions.prompt: Elevation of a hiker who starts at sea level and descends 250 feet.
questions.explanation: Worked example: hiker starts at sea level (0) and descends 250 feet (6.NS.C.5). New elevation = 0 + (−250) = −250 feet. Common student error: writing 250 ft (ignoring direction) or −0 (formless). Strategy: 'descends' or 'below sea level' = negative. 'Ascends' or 'above sea level' = positive. Elevation 0 = sea level; below is negative, above is positive. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C uses elevation as a real-world integer context.

Worked example: absolute value of −12 (6.NS.C.7c). |−12| = 12. Absolute value strips the sign, giving the distance from 0. Common student error: writing −12 (didn't strip sign) or 0. Strategy: |x| = x if x ≥ 0; |x| = −x if x < 0. In words: drop the sign and report the magnitude. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.7c introduces absolute value formally and uses it for distance computations. Algebraically: |x| is defined as x if x ≥ 0, and −x if x < 0. So |−12| = −(−12) = 12. Always positive. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.7c expects fluent absolute-value evaluation.

questions.prompt: Height difference between a hiker at 350 ft and a diver at −80 ft.
questions.explanation: Worked example: hiker at 350 ft, diver at −80 ft — height difference (6.NS.C.5). Difference = 350 − (−80) = 350 + 80 = 430 feet. Common student error: writing 270 (treating −80 as 80 and subtracting) or 430 with wrong reasoning. Strategy: 'difference' for elevation problems = highest minus lowest. Highest = 350; lowest = −80. Difference = 350 − (−80) = 430 ft. Always check: 'subtracting a negative' = adding a positive.

Worked example: distance from (3, −2) to (3, 5) (6.NS.C.8). Same x (3), so vertical distance. |5 − (−2)| = |5 + 2| = 7 units. Common student error: writing 3 (subtracting in wrong direction) or −7. Strategy: axis-aligned distance = subtract the differing coordinate and take absolute value. Always take ABSOLUTE VALUE — distance is never negative. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.8 expects this 'same x or same y' shortcut for distance.

Worked example: x + 5 = 12 (6.EE.B.7). To isolate x, SUBTRACT 5 from both sides: x + 5 − 5 = 12 − 5 → x = 7. Common student error: writing x = 17 (adding 5 instead of subtracting) or x = 12. Strategy: do the INVERSE operation to isolate the variable. Addition's inverse is subtraction. Always do the same operation on BOTH SIDES to maintain balance. Verify: 7 + 5 = 12 ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.7 introduces one-step equation solving.

Worked example: x − 4 = 9 (6.EE.B.7). To isolate x, ADD 4 to both sides: x − 4 + 4 = 9 + 4 → x = 13. Common student error: writing x = 5 (subtracting instead of adding) or x = 9. Strategy: subtraction's inverse is addition. Add the same amount to both sides to undo the subtraction. Verify: 13 − 4 = 9 ✓. Always check your solution in the original equation. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.7 expects fluency with this type. Always do the same operation on BOTH SIDES to maintain balance — like a scale.

Worked example: 3x = 24 (6.EE.B.7). To isolate x, DIVIDE both sides by 3: 3x ÷ 3 = 24 ÷ 3 → x = 8. Common student error: writing x = 21 (subtracting instead of dividing) or x = 24 ÷ 6. Strategy: multiplication's inverse is division. Divide both sides by the coefficient (the number multiplying x). Verify: 3 x 8 = 24 ✓. Always perform the same operation on both sides. The coefficient 3 means x is being multiplied by 3. To undo multiplication, divide. Both sides must be divided by the same number.

Worked example: x/5 = 6 (6.EE.B.7). To isolate x, MULTIPLY both sides by 5: (x/5) x 5 = 6 x 5 → x = 30. Common student error: writing x = 11 (adding 5 instead of multiplying) or x = 1.2. Strategy: division's inverse is multiplication. Multiply both sides by the divisor (the number you're dividing by). Verify: 30 ÷ 5 = 6 ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.7 expects fluency with all four basic one-step equations.

Worked example: 7 + x = 7 + 3 (6.EE.B.7). The right side simplifies to 10: 7 + x = 10. Subtract 7 from both sides: x = 3. OR notice that both sides have +7, so x must equal 3. Common student error: writing x = 7 (matching the wrong term) or x = 10. Strategy: simplify each side first if possible, then isolate the variable. Or use matching: 7 + ___ = 7 + 3 forces the blank to be 3. The matching strategy works whenever both sides have a shared term: cancel the shared term and solve the simpler equation.

Worked example: in 5x, the COEFFICIENT is 5 (6.EE.A.2b). The coefficient is the number multiplying a variable. Common student error: writing x (the variable) or 1. Strategy: in a term like 5x, the number part is the coefficient, the letter part is the variable. The coefficient indicates how many copies of the variable. If no number appears, the coefficient is implicitly 1 (so x means 1x). NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.2b introduces algebraic vocabulary.

Worked example: 'a number n plus 7' as expression (6.EE.A.2a). 'Plus 7' = add 7, so the expression is n + 7. Common student error: writing 7n (multiplying instead of adding) or n − 7. Strategy: translate keywords carefully. 'PLUS' or 'sum' or 'more than' = ADD (+). 'MINUS' or 'less than' or 'difference' = SUBTRACT (−). 'TIMES' or 'product' = MULTIPLY (x). 'DIVIDED BY' or 'quotient' = DIVIDE (÷). The simpler operation usually wins for translation. Always identify the operation FIRST, then the operands. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.2a is the bridge from arithmetic to algebra.

questions.prompt: Expression for '3 times the sum of x and 4.'.
questions.explanation: Worked example: '3 times the sum of x and 4' (6.EE.A.2a). The sum (x + 4) goes in parentheses; '3 times' multiplies the whole sum: 3(x + 4). Common student error: writing 3x + 4 (only multiplying x by 3) or 3 + x + 4. Strategy: 'the sum of' = parenthesized addition. 'Times' that sum = multiplied by 3. Always use parentheses for 'the sum of' or 'the difference of' before applying outside operations.

Worked example: 4x − 3 at x = 2 (6.EE.A.2c). Substitute x = 2: 4(2) − 3 = 8 − 3 = 5. Common student error: writing 4 − 3 = 1 (forgetting to substitute) or 4x − 3 = 5 without showing work. Strategy: when evaluating expressions, REPLACE the variable with its given value in parentheses, then simplify using order of operations (PEMDAS). NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.2c expects fluent evaluation. The substitution must replace EVERY occurrence of x. If the expression had two x's (like 2x + 3x), substitute in both: 2(2) + 3(2) = 4 + 6 = 10.

Worked example: distributive property: 3(x + 5) (6.EE.A.3). Distribute 3 to each term inside: 3 x x + 3 x 5 = 3x + 15. Common student error: writing 3x + 5 (didn't distribute to 5) or 8x. Strategy: the distributive property says a(b + c) = ab + ac. Multiply the outside factor by EVERY term inside. Verify: 3(x + 5) at x = 2 gives 3 x 7 = 21; 3x + 15 at x = 2 gives 6 + 15 = 21 ✓. The distributive property is also written 'a times the sum of b and c equals a times b plus a times c.' NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.3 uses this to generate equivalent expressions.

Worked example: 3 + 5 = 5 + 3 (6.EE.A.3). This is the COMMUTATIVE property of addition: order doesn't matter. Common student error: writing 'associative' (which deals with grouping). Strategy: COMMUTATIVE = order swap, a + b = b + a or ab = ba. ASSOCIATIVE = grouping change, (a+b)+c = a+(b+c). DISTRIBUTIVE = a(b+c) = ab+ac. Identity, inverse, and zero are other key properties. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.3 expects students to name these properties.

Worked example: (2 + 3) + 4 = 2 + (3 + 4) (6.EE.A.3). This is the ASSOCIATIVE property of addition: regrouping doesn't change the sum. Common student error: writing 'commutative' (which deals with order). Strategy: ASSOCIATIVE means parentheses can MOVE without changing the result: (a+b)+c = a+(b+c) or (ab)c = a(bc). It works for addition and multiplication, NOT for subtraction or division. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.3 expects students to identify properties by name. Associative property works for + and x but NOT for − or ÷, because subtraction and division aren't associative.

Worked example: a x 1 = a (6.EE.A.3). This is the IDENTITY property of multiplication: any number times 1 equals itself. Common student error: writing 'zero property' (which is a x 0 = 0). Strategy: IDENTITY for multiplication = a x 1 = a. IDENTITY for addition = a + 0 = a. ZERO property of multiplication = a x 0 = 0. These three properties are fundamental and appear in every algebraic simplification.

Worked example: a(b + c) = ab + ac (6.EE.A.3). This is the DISTRIBUTIVE property: multiplication 'distributes' over addition. Common student error: writing 'associative' (which deals with grouping a+b+c). Strategy: distributive property links multiplication and addition. The factor outside parentheses multiplies EACH term inside. Reverse direction (factoring): ab + ac = a(b + c). NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.3 uses distributive property to generate equivalent expressions.

Worked example: in 4x + 3 + 2x, the LIKE TERMS are 4x and 2x (6.EE.A.4). 'Like terms' have the SAME variable raised to the SAME power. Both 4x and 2x have x to the first power. The constant 3 is a separate kind of term (a constant). Common student error: combining 3 with 4x or 2x. Strategy: like terms can be COMBINED by adding their coefficients: 4x + 2x = 6x. So the simplified expression is 6x + 3. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.4 introduces combining like terms.

Worked example: symbol for 'less than' (6.EE.B.8). The symbol '<' means 'less than.' So 'x is less than 5' = x < 5. Common student error: writing '>' (greater than) or '≤' (less than or equal to). Strategy: memorize the four inequality symbols: < (less than), > (greater than), ≤ (less than or equal to), ≥ (greater than or equal to). Read left to right: 'a < b' means 'a is less than b.' Mnemonic: the OPEN side of < or > points to the LARGER number.

questions.prompt: Inequality for 'x is greater than or equal to 7.'.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 'x is greater than or equal to 7' (6.EE.B.8). Symbol for 'greater than or equal to' = ≥. So x ≥ 7. Common student error: writing x > 7 (strict inequality, excludes 7) or x = 7. Strategy: 'or equal to' phrases require the ≤ or ≥ symbol (with the line underneath). 'Strict' inequalities (just > or <) don't include the boundary. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.8 expects students to translate inequality language to symbols.

questions.prompt: Solution set: x > 5 on a number line.
questions.explanation: Worked example: graph x > 5 on a number line (6.EE.B.8). Place an OPEN CIRCLE at 5 (since the inequality is strict — 5 is NOT included), and shade everything to the RIGHT (since x is greater than 5). Common student error: drawing a closed circle (treats > as ≥) or shading left. Strategy: OPEN circle for < or > (strict); CLOSED circle for ≤ or ≥ (includes the boundary). Shade RIGHT for > or ≥; LEFT for < or ≤. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.8 introduces graphing inequalities.

questions.prompt: Value(s) that satisfy x < 4 from the set {2, 3, 4, 5}.
questions.explanation: Worked example: which values satisfy x < 4 from {2, 3, 4, 5} (6.EE.B.8). Test each: 2 < 4 ✓; 3 < 4 ✓; 4 < 4 ✗ (4 is NOT less than itself); 5 < 4 ✗. So 2 and 3 satisfy the inequality. Common student error: including 4 (treating < as ≤) or excluding 3. Strategy: strict inequalities exclude equality. Test each value in the inequality; keep only those that make it TRUE. Verify each kept value satisfies the inequality. For x < 4, the integer solutions from this set are {2, 3} since 4 fails (not strictly less than 4).

questions.prompt: Inequality for 'I have at most $20 to spend.'.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 'I have at most $20 to spend' (6.EE.B.8). 'At most' means 'no more than' = ≤. So x ≤ 20 where x is the amount spent. Common student error: writing x < 20 (excludes $20, but $20 is allowed) or x ≥ 20. Strategy: 'at most' and 'no more than' = ≤. 'At least' and 'no less than' = ≥. 'More than' = > (strict). 'Less than' = < (strict). NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.8 tests these everyday inequality phrases.

questions.prompt: Equation for 'I have x cookies and you give me 5 more for a total of 12.'.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 'I have x cookies, get 5 more, total 12' (6.EE.B.7). Translate: current + added = total → x + 5 = 12. Common student error: writing 5 + 12 = x (wrong setup) or x − 5 = 12. Strategy: identify the UNKNOWN as x. Translate words to equation: 'have x' = x, 'get 5 more' = +5, 'total of 12' = = 12. Always check the structure matches the wording. Always check the structure matches the wording. The unknown 'cookies I have' is x; '+5' represents the cookies received; '=12' is the final total.

Worked example: solve x + 5 = 12 (6.EE.B.7). Subtract 5 from both sides: x = 12 − 5 = 7. Common student error: writing x = 17 (adding instead of subtracting) or x = 12. Strategy: addition's inverse is subtraction. To isolate x in x + 5 = 12, subtract 5 from both sides. Verify: 7 + 5 = 12 ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.7 expects fluent solving of one-step equations. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.7 expects fluent solving of one-step equations. Quick check: substitute x = 7 back into the original: 7 + 5 = 12 ✓.

questions.prompt: Number of pencils per friend if 24 pencils are equally shared among 6.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 24 pencils ÷ 6 friends (6.EE.B.7). Equation: 6x = 24 (where x = pencils per friend). Divide: x = 24 ÷ 6 = 4. Common student error: writing x = 24 − 6 = 18 (wrong operation) or x = 6/24. Strategy: 'shared equally' = division. Total ÷ number of groups = amount per group. Set up an equation with the unknown as 'per group' value. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.7 uses equal-share problems for division equations.

questions.prompt: Equation for 'twice a number minus 7 equals 13.'.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 'twice a number minus 7 equals 13' (6.EE.B.6/B.7). Let x = number. 'Twice a number' = 2x. 'Minus 7' = −7. 'Equals 13' = =13. Equation: 2x − 7 = 13. Common student error: writing 2(x − 7) = 13 (wrong grouping) or 2x + 7 = 13. Strategy: translate phrase by phrase. 'Twice a number' applies to JUST the number; 'minus 7' subtracts 7 from the doubled number. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.6 introduces two-step expressions.

questions.prompt: Cost of one pencil if 4 pencils cost $1.20.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 4 pencils cost $1.20 — cost per pencil (6.RP.A.3b/6.EE.B.7). Equation: 4x = 1.20 where x = cost per pencil. Solve: x = 1.20 ÷ 4 = 0.30. So $0.30 per pencil. Common student error: writing $4.80 (multiplying instead) or $1.16. Strategy: total cost ÷ number of items = cost per item. Set up an equation and use one-step solving. Always include units (dollars). NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3b uses unit-price problems for proportional reasoning.

questions.prompt: Area of a rectangle 8 cm by 5 cm.
questions.explanation: Worked example: rectangle 8 cm by 5 cm (6.G.A.1). Area = length x width = 8 x 5 = 40 sq cm. Common student error: writing 13 cm (adding) or 26 cm (perimeter). Strategy: rectangle area is ALWAYS length x width. Units are SQUARED (sq cm, cm²). Don't confuse with perimeter (2L + 2W). Always include square units in the answer. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.1 expects fluent area computation for rectangles and other polygons.

questions.prompt: Area of a triangle with base 10 m and height 6 m.
questions.explanation: Worked example: triangle base 10 m, height 6 m (6.G.A.1). Area = (1/2) x base x height = (1/2) x 10 x 6 = 30 sq m. Common student error: writing 60 sq m (forgetting the 1/2) or 16 (adding). Strategy: triangle area = ½ x base x height. The 1/2 reflects that a triangle is half of a rectangle with the same base and height. Always include square units. Base and height must be PERPENDICULAR. The 1/2 factor reflects that a triangle is exactly half of a parallelogram with the same base and height. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.1 expects fluent triangle area.

questions.prompt: Area of a parallelogram with base 12 in and height 5 in.
questions.explanation: Worked example: parallelogram base 12 in, height 5 in (6.G.A.1). Area = base x height = 12 x 5 = 60 sq in. Common student error: confusing slant side with height (use perpendicular height only) or writing 30 sq in (using triangle formula). Strategy: parallelogram area = base x height, where height is PERPENDICULAR to the base (not the slant side). It's the same formula as a rectangle because you can rearrange a parallelogram into a rectangle.

questions.prompt: Area of a trapezoid with parallel sides 6 and 10, height 4.
questions.explanation: Worked example: trapezoid with parallel sides 6 and 10, height 4 (6.G.A.1). Area = (1/2)(b₁ + b₂)h = (1/2)(6 + 10)(4) = (1/2)(16)(4) = 32 sq units. Common student error: writing 60 (multiplying without 1/2) or 80. Strategy: trapezoid area = (1/2) x (sum of parallel sides) x height. The two parallel sides are the 'bases'; height is perpendicular distance between them. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.1 expects fluency with trapezoid area.

questions.prompt: Area of a composite shape made of a rectangle (4 x 6) and a triangle on top (base 4, height 3).
questions.explanation: Worked example: rectangle (4x6) + triangle on top (base 4, height 3) (6.G.A.1). Rectangle area = 4 x 6 = 24. Triangle area = (1/2)(4)(3) = 6. Composite area = 24 + 6 = 30 sq units. Common student error: writing 24 (only computing rectangle) or 36 (treating it all as a rectangle). Strategy: composite shapes break into rectangles and triangles. Compute each area separately, then SUM. Always include square units. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.1 tests composite areas.

questions.prompt: Volume of a rectangular prism 2 x 3 x 4 units.
questions.explanation: Worked example: rectangular prism 2 x 3 x 4 (6.G.A.2). Volume = length x width x height = 2 x 3 x 4 = 24 cubic units. Common student error: writing 9 (adding dimensions) or 24 sq units (wrong units). Strategy: rectangular prism volume = lwh, units are CUBIC. Visualize: a 2 x 3 base holds 6 cubes per layer; 4 layers tall = 24 cubes total. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.2 extends Grade 5 volume to non-integer dimensions.

questions.prompt: Surface area of a cube with side 3.
questions.explanation: Worked example: cube with side 3 — surface area (6.G.A.4). A cube has 6 faces, each a square with area s² = 9. Total SA = 6 x 9 = 54 sq units. Common student error: writing 27 (volume instead of SA) or 36 (only 4 faces). Strategy: surface area = sum of all face areas. For a cube, all 6 faces are equal, so SA = 6s². Don't confuse with volume (s³). Units are SQUARED for SA, CUBIC for volume. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.4 expects students to compute surface area using nets or by adding face areas. Don't confuse with volume.

questions.prompt: Volume of a prism with base area 12 cm² and height 4 cm.
questions.explanation: Worked example: prism with base area 12 cm² and height 4 cm (6.G.A.2). Volume = base area x height = 12 x 4 = 48 cubic cm. Common student error: writing 16 cm³ (adding) or 48 sq cm (wrong units). Strategy: 'base times height' formula applies to ANY prism, not just rectangular. The 'base' is the 2D shape on top/bottom; height is the perpendicular distance between the two bases. Volume units are CUBIC.

questions.prompt: Surface area of a rectangular prism 2 x 3 x 4.
questions.explanation: Worked example: rectangular prism 2 x 3 x 4 — surface area (6.G.A.4). 6 faces in 3 pairs: 2x3=6 (top/bottom, two faces: 12), 2x4=8 (front/back: 16), 3x4=12 (sides: 24). Total: 12 + 16 + 24 = 52 sq units. Common student error: writing 24 (volume) or 26 (only one of each face). Strategy: SA of rectangular prism = 2(lw + lh + wh). Compute the three distinct face areas, add, then double. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.4 expects fluent surface area for rectangular prisms. Use a net (the 'unfolded' prism) to visualize all six faces.

questions.prompt: Volume of a triangular prism with triangular base area 8 and length 5.
questions.explanation: Worked example: triangular prism, base area 8, length 5 (6.G.A.2). Volume = base area x length = 8 x 5 = 40 cubic units. Common student error: writing 13 (adding) or 80. Strategy: prism volume = base area x height (or length). For a triangular prism, the 'base' is the triangle. Compute the triangle's area first (here given as 8), then multiply by the prism's length. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.2 extends volume to triangular and other prisms.

Worked example: median of 3, 7, 8, 11, 15 (6.SP.A.3 / 6.SP.B.5). Data already in order; 5 values, so middle (3rd) = 8. Common student error: writing 7 (off by one) or 9 (mean instead of median). Strategy: median = middle value when sorted. For odd count n, median is the (n+1)/2-th value. For even count, median = average of two middle values. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent median computation. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent median computation. For data with an even count, the median is the average of the two middle values.

questions.prompt: Type of graph that uses a single bar divided into a 5-number summary.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 5-number summary represented in a BOX PLOT (6.SP.B.4). Box plot shows min, Q1, median, Q3, max as a divided rectangle with whiskers. Common student error: writing 'bar graph' or 'histogram.' Strategy: box plots specifically display the 5-number summary: minimum (left whisker end), Q1 (left edge of box), median (line in box), Q3 (right edge of box), maximum (right whisker end). NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.4 introduces box plots as a data-display tool.

Worked example: mode of 2, 3, 3, 5, 7 (6.SP.B.5). Mode = most frequent value. 3 appears twice; others appear once. So mode = 3. Common student error: writing 5 (median) or 4 (mean). Strategy: mode is the value that appears MOST OFTEN. A data set can have one mode, multiple modes (bimodal, multimodal), or no mode (if all values are unique). NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects mode alongside mean and median.

Worked example: range of 12, 15, 7, 22, 10 (6.SP.B.5). Range = max − min = 22 − 7 = 15. Common student error: writing 22 (just the max) or 10 (a value in the middle). Strategy: range measures the spread of data — the simplest spread statistic. Range = highest − lowest. Always identify min and max first, then subtract. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 introduces range as a measure of variability. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 introduces range as the simplest measure of spread. Range tells the total 'width' of the data set.

Worked example: MAD of 3, 5, 5, 7 with mean 5 (6.SP.B.5). Compute |each value − mean|: |3−5|=2, |5−5|=0, |5−5|=0, |7−5|=2. Sum = 4. Divide by n: 4 ÷ 4 = 1. So MAD = 1. Common student error: forgetting to take absolute values (giving 0) or dividing by wrong count. Strategy: Mean Absolute Deviation = average distance from the mean. Take |value − mean| for each data point, sum, divide by n. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 introduces MAD.

questions.prompt: Type of plot that uses x's stacked over a number line.
questions.explanation: Worked example: type of plot using x's stacked over a number line (6.SP.B.4). This is a DOT PLOT (also called a line plot). Each data value is shown as a dot (or x) above its value on the number line. Common student error: writing 'bar graph' (which uses bars, not dots) or 'histogram' (which groups data into intervals). Strategy: dot plot = individual data points shown one-by-one above a number line; works best for small data sets.

questions.prompt: 5-number summary for the data 2, 5, 6, 8, 10, 13, 15 in order.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 5-number summary of 2, 5, 6, 8, 10, 13, 15 (6.SP.B.4/B.5). Min = 2, Max = 15. Median (4th of 7) = 8. Lower half: 2, 5, 6 → Q1 = 5. Upper half: 10, 13, 15 → Q3 = 13. Five-number summary: 2, 5, 8, 13, 15. Common student error: including the median in Q1/Q3 calculation. Strategy: order the data, find min, max, median, then Q1 (median of lower half) and Q3 (median of upper half). NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.4 expects fluent 5-number summary computation, used to build box plots. Always order the data first before finding Q1, median, and Q3.

questions.prompt: Graph type that groups data into intervals.
questions.explanation: Worked example: graph that groups data into intervals (6.SP.B.4). This is a HISTOGRAM — like a bar graph, but bars represent counts of data falling in each interval (bin). Common student error: writing 'bar graph' (separate categories) or 'dot plot' (individual values). Strategy: histograms show CONTINUOUS data grouped into intervals; bar graphs show CATEGORICAL data. Histogram bars touch; bar-graph bars have gaps. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.4 distinguishes these display types.

questions.prompt: Symmetric, skewed-right, or skewed-left description for data 2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5.
questions.explanation: Worked example: distribution of 2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5 (6.SP.B.4/B.5). Center value 4 has highest count; the distribution is mirror-image symmetric around 4: (2, 5), (3, 3), (4×3). This is SYMMETRIC. Common student error: writing 'skewed right' (tail on right — not the case here) or 'random.' Strategy: a symmetric distribution has equal frequencies on both sides of the center. Skewed-right (positive skew) has a long right tail; skewed-left has a long left tail.

questions.prompt: IQR for data with Q1 = 5 and Q3 = 13.
questions.explanation: Worked example: IQR with Q1 = 5 and Q3 = 13 (6.SP.B.5). IQR = Q3 − Q1 = 13 − 5 = 8. Common student error: writing 18 (adding) or 13 (just Q3). Strategy: Interquartile Range = Q3 − Q1 = the spread of the middle 50% of the data. IQR is a robust measure of spread, less sensitive to outliers than range. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects IQR computation alongside range and MAD. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 introduces IQR as a robust spread measure, especially useful with skewed data or outliers where range can mislead.

Worked example: mean of 4, 6, 8, 10 (6.SP.B.5). Sum = 4 + 6 + 8 + 10 = 28. Divide by count 4: 28 ÷ 4 = 7. Common student error: writing 7 with no method shown, or 8 (median). Strategy: mean = sum of all values ÷ count. Add all values, divide by n. Mean is sensitive to outliers (extreme values shift it). NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent mean calculation. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent mean calculation. Mean is sometimes called the 'arithmetic average' or simply 'average.'

questions.prompt: Is 'What is the median height of students in this class?' a statistical question? (Yes or No).
questions.explanation: Worked example: 'median height of students in this class' — statistical question? (6.SP.A.1). YES — the question anticipates VARIABILITY in heights across students. Common student error: saying 'no' because we can compute the answer. Strategy: a STATISTICAL question is one that anticipates variability and is answered by collecting data. 'What is the median height in this class?' expects different heights and uses statistics to summarize them.

questions.prompt: Is 'How tall am I?' a statistical question?
questions.explanation: Worked example: 'How tall am I?' — statistical question? (6.SP.A.1). NO — this question has a SINGLE answer (your height); no variability is expected. Common student error: saying 'yes' because height varies across people. Strategy: a statistical question asks about a GROUP and anticipates variability ACROSS the group. A non-statistical question asks about a single individual or a single fact. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.A.1 directly tests this distinction.

questions.prompt: Measure of center for 'typical' value: mean or range?
questions.explanation: Worked example: measure of center for 'typical value' — mean or range? (6.SP.A.2/A.3). MEAN. Range measures SPREAD, not center. Common student error: writing 'range' (a spread statistic). Strategy: measures of center include MEAN, MEDIAN, and MODE. Measures of spread/variability include RANGE, IQR, and MAD. 'Typical value' = center. 'How varied' = spread. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.A.2 emphasizes this distinction.

Worked example: median of 4, 5, 7, 8 (6.SP.B.5). 4 values (even count). Middle pair = 5 and 7. Median = average of pair = (5 + 7)/2 = 6. Common student error: writing 5 or 7 (one of the middle values) or 6.5. Strategy: when n is EVEN, median = average of the two middle values. When n is ODD, median is the single middle value. Always sort data first. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 tests median for both even and odd counts.

Worked example: simplify 12:18 (6.RP.A.3a). GCF of 12 and 18 is 6. Divide both parts by 6: 12 ÷ 6 = 2; 18 ÷ 6 = 3. So 12:18 = 2:3. Common student error: writing 12:18 unsimplified or 6:9 (only divided by 2). Strategy: simplify ratios just like fractions — divide both parts by the GCF. Always express ratios in lowest terms unless asked otherwise. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3a expects simplest-form ratios. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3a expects simplest-form ratios as the standard answer. Verify: 2:3 cannot be reduced further because 2 and 3 share no common factor besides 1.

Worked example: $8 for 4 pounds — unit rate (6.RP.A.2). Unit rate = total ÷ quantity = $8 ÷ 4 = $2 per pound. Common student error: writing $4 per pound (dividing wrong way) or $32. Strategy: unit rate = cost per ONE unit. Divide total by quantity. 'Per' = division. Always check units: dollars ÷ pounds = dollars/pound ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.2 expects fluent unit-rate computation. The 'per' keyword always signals division. Quick check: at $2/lb, 4 lbs cost 4 x $2 = $8 ✓.

Worked example: constant of proportionality for (1, 4) and (2, 8) (6.RP.A.3). y/x for each: 4/1 = 4; 8/2 = 4. Constant k = 4. Equation: y = 4x. Common student error: writing 4 and 8 (just listing y-values) or 1 and 2. Strategy: in a proportional relationship, k = y/x is constant across all points. Compute y/x for each given point; if all match, that's k. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3 expects identifying k from tables, graphs, or coordinate pairs.

Worked example: 15% of 80 (6.RP.A.3c). Method 1: 0.15 x 80 = 12. Method 2: 10% of 80 = 8; 5% of 80 = 4; sum = 12. Common student error: writing 8 (only computing 10%) or 1.2.
