Strategy: 'percent of a number' = decimal x number. 15% = 0.15. For mental math, split 15% as 10% + 5% (half of 10%) for easier computation. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c uses 'percent of a number' as the standard percent application. Memorize key conversions: 10% = ÷10, 20% = ÷5, 25% = ÷4, 50% = ÷2.

Worked example: percent change from 50 to 75 (6.RP.A.3c). Change = 75 − 50 = 25. Percent change = change ÷ ORIGINAL = 25 ÷ 50 = 0.5 = 50%. So 50% increase. Common student error: dividing by new value (25/75 = 33%) or by difference. Strategy: percent change = (new − old) ÷ OLD x 100. Denominator is always the ORIGINAL starting value, not the new one. The base for percent change is ALWAYS the original (older) value, not the new one. NYS Grade 6 6.RP.A.3c is precise about this distinction.

Worked example: opposite of 8 (6.NS.C.6). Opposite flips the sign: 8 → −8. Common student error: writing 8 (didn't flip) or 1/8 (reciprocal). Strategy: 'opposite' means same magnitude, opposite sign. Opposite of any positive is negative; opposite of any negative is positive. Opposite of 0 is 0. Don't confuse opposite (sign flip) with reciprocal (top-bottom flip). Visual: on a number line, 8 is 8 units RIGHT of 0; its opposite −8 is 8 units LEFT of 0. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.6 introduces opposites alongside absolute value.

Worked example: coefficient of 7x (6.EE.A.2b). The coefficient is the number multiplying the variable, which is 7. Common student error: writing x (variable) or 1. Strategy: in a term like 7x, the number part is the coefficient and the letter is the variable. The coefficient tells how many copies of the variable. If no number appears before the variable (just x), the coefficient is implicitly 1. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.2b expects students to identify coefficients in any algebraic term.

Worked example: 4/5 ÷ 2/3 (6.NS.A.1). Use KCF: 4/5 ÷ 2/3 = 4/5 x 3/2 = 12/10 = 6/5. Common student error: writing 8/15 (multiplying without flipping). Strategy: dividing fractions uses 'keep, change, flip' (KCF). Keep the first fraction, change ÷ to x, flip the second. Multiply across, then simplify. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.A.1 introduces fraction division. Verify: (4/5)(3/2) = 12/10 = 6/5. Reverse-check by multiplying: 6/5 x 2/3 = 12/15 = 4/5 ✓. Dividing by 2/3 gives a larger result than 4/5 because 2/3 < 1.

Worked example: GCF of 24 and 36 (6.NS.B.4). Prime factorize: 24 = 2³·3; 36 = 2²·3². Common primes at lowest power: 2² · 3 = 4 x 3 = 12. So GCF(24,36) = 12. Common student error: writing 72 (LCM) or 6. Strategy: GCF = product of shared primes at LOWEST power. Use prime factorization for reliability. Verify: 12 divides both 24 and 36 evenly ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.B.4 expects fluent GCF/LCM up to two-digit numbers. Use GCF to simplify fractions and LCM to find common denominators for adding fractions.

Worked example: −5 + 3 (6.NS.C.5). Different signs — subtract absolute values: |−5| − |3| = 5 − 3 = 2. Take sign of larger absolute value (−5): result = −2. Common student error: writing −8 (adding magnitudes) or 8. Strategy: 'same sign' add absolute values keep sign; 'different signs' subtract absolute values take sign of larger absolute value. Number-line check: start at −5, move right 3 → −2 ✓.

Worked example: 3x + 2 at x = 5 (6.EE.A.2c). Substitute: 3(5) + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17. Common student error: writing 35 + 2 (concatenating) or 3 + 2 = 5 (forgetting to substitute). Strategy: when evaluating, REPLACE the variable with its value in parentheses, then use PEMDAS. Multiplication before addition: 3 x 5 first, then + 2. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.2c expects fluent expression evaluation. Always substitute the value in parentheses, then apply PEMDAS to simplify carefully.

Worked example: 2(x + 3) using distributive (6.EE.A.3). Distribute: 2 x x + 2 x 3 = 2x + 6. Common student error: writing 2x + 3 (didn't distribute to 3) or 2 + 2x. Strategy: a(b + c) = ab + ac. Multiply the outside factor by EVERY term inside the parentheses. Verify: 2(x + 3) at x = 1 = 2 x 4 = 8; 2x + 6 at x = 1 = 2 + 6 = 8 ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.A.3 uses the distributive property as a key tool for generating equivalent expressions. Reverse direction is 'factoring,' covered in Grade 7.

Worked example: x − 8 = 12 (6.EE.B.7). Add 8 to both sides: x − 8 + 8 = 12 + 8 → x = 20. Common student error: writing x = 4 (subtracted instead) or x = 12. Strategy: subtraction's inverse is addition. To isolate x in x − k = c, add k to both sides. Verify: 20 − 8 = 12 ✓. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.7 expects fluent solving of one-step equations using inverse operations. Always check by substituting the answer back into the original equation. For x = 20: 20 − 8 = 12 ✓.

Worked example: 7 − (−3) (6.NS.C.5). Subtracting a negative is adding a positive: 7 − (−3) = 7 + 3 = 10. Common student error: writing 4 (treating as 7 − 3) or −10. Strategy: two negatives make a positive: − − = +. So a − (−b) = a + b. This converts integer subtraction problems into integer addition. NYS Grade 6 6.NS.C.5 expects fluency with this rule. Number-line check: starting at 7 and 'walking away from −3' (right) moves you to 10. This is the same as 7 + 3 = 10.

Worked example: 'no more than 50' as inequality (6.EE.B.8). 'No more than' means ≤. So x ≤ 50. Common student error: writing x < 50 (excludes 50) or x ≥ 50. Strategy: phrase keywords for inequalities — 'at most' / 'no more than' = ≤; 'at least' / 'no less than' = ≥; 'more than' = > (strict); 'less than' = < (strict). 'No more' includes the boundary value. NYS Grade 6 6.EE.B.8 tests these everyday inequality phrases. 'No more' or 'at most' INCLUDES the boundary; 'less than' or 'more than' EXCLUDES it.

Worked example: rectangle 5 x 8 (6.G.A.1). Area = length x width = 5 x 8 = 40 sq units. Common student error: writing 13 (adding) or 26 (perimeter). Strategy: rectangle area = lw, units are SQUARED. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.1 expects fluent rectangle area, leading into triangle and parallelogram formulas. Don't confuse area (square units) with perimeter (linear units). NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.1 expects fluent rectangle area computation as a foundation for more complex polygons.

questions.prompt: Area of a triangle base 6 height 4.
questions.explanation: Worked example: triangle base 6, height 4 (6.G.A.1). Area = (1/2) x base x height = (1/2)(6)(4) = 12 sq units. Common student error: writing 24 (forgot 1/2) or 10 (added). Strategy: triangle area = ½bh. The 1/2 reflects that a triangle is half of a parallelogram with the same base and height. Always include square units. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.1 expects fluent triangle area. The 1/2 factor distinguishes triangle area from parallelogram area, since a triangle is half a parallelogram.

Worked example: cube with side 4 — volume (6.G.A.2). V = s³ = 4³ = 4 x 4 x 4 = 64 cubic units. Common student error: writing 12 (adding 4+4+4) or 16 (only 4²). Strategy: cube volume = side cubed (s³). For any rectangular prism, V = lwh; cube is a special case with l = w = h = s. Quick check: a 4 x 4 x 4 cube holds 4 layers of 16 unit cubes = 64 unit cubes total. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.2 expects fluent prism volume.

Worked example: 2x3x4 box — surface area (6.G.A.4). Three pairs: 2x3 = 6 (top/bottom = 12), 2x4 = 8 (front/back = 16), 3x4 = 12 (sides = 24). Total: 12 + 16 + 24 = 52 sq units. Common student error: writing 24 (volume) or 26. Strategy: SA = 2(lw + lh + wh). Add areas of all six faces, or compute three distinct face areas and double the total. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.4 expects students to use a net of the prism to add up all six face areas. Always include square units, since surface area is two-dimensional.

questions.prompt: Area of trapezoid bases 4 and 6, height 5.
questions.explanation: Worked example: trapezoid bases 4 and 6, height 5 (6.G.A.1). Area = (1/2)(b₁ + b₂)h = (1/2)(4 + 6)(5) = (1/2)(10)(5) = 25 sq units. Common student error: writing 50 (forgot 1/2) or 30 (used only one base). Strategy: trapezoid area = ½(sum of parallel sides) x height. The bases are the parallel sides; height is perpendicular distance between them. NYS Grade 6 6.G.A.1 expects fluent trapezoid area computation. Always verify the height is perpendicular to the two parallel bases, not the slanted sides.

Worked example: mean of 5, 10, 15 (6.SP.B.5). Sum = 30. Divide by count 3: 30 ÷ 3 = 10. Common student error: writing 15 (the max) or 7 (no method). Strategy: mean = sum ÷ count. Add all values, divide by n. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent mean computation. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent mean computation. Mean is the 'fair-share' value — if everyone got the same amount, it would be the mean. Sensitive to outliers.

Worked example: median of 1, 4, 7, 9, 11 (6.SP.B.5). Already sorted; 5 values, middle (3rd) = 7. Common student error: writing 4 (off by one) or 6 (mean approx). Strategy: median = middle value when sorted. For odd n, the (n+1)/2-th value is the median. For even n, average the two middle values. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent median computation. For odd n, the median is the single middle value. For even n, average the two middle values. Always sort first.

Worked example: range of 12, 15, 7, 22, 10 (6.SP.B.5). Range = max − min = 22 − 7 = 15. Common student error: writing 22 (just max) or 12 (a middle value). Strategy: range = highest − lowest. Find min and max first, then subtract. Range measures spread/variability of data. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 introduces range as the simplest measure of spread. Range tells the total 'width' of the data set: from lowest to highest.

Worked example: mode of 4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 6 (6.SP.B.5). Count each: 4 appears 2x; 5 appears 1x; 6 appears 3x. Most frequent = 6. Common student error: writing 4 (first repeat) or 5 (median). Strategy: mode = most frequent value. A data set may have one mode, multiple modes (bimodal), or no mode if all values appear equally. NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent mode computation. A data set can have multiple modes (bimodal, multimodal) if two or more values tie for most frequent.

Worked example: IQR with Q1 = 10, Q3 = 22 (6.SP.B.5). IQR = Q3 − Q1 = 22 − 10 = 12. Common student error: writing 32 (adding) or 22 (just Q3). Strategy: Interquartile Range = Q3 − Q1 = spread of middle 50%. IQR is a robust spread measure (less affected by outliers than range). NYS Grade 6 6.SP.B.5 expects fluent IQR computation. IQR is a robust spread measure, less affected by outliers than range. Used in box-plot construction.
